coredata.dc:title: Employing phosphorescent carbon dots@silica for glutathione sensing with low background interference
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Xinyi
Liang, Wanqing
Gao, Yawen
Shi, Yongqin
Zhang, Cheng
Zhang, Kui
coredata.dc:description: Glutathione (GSH) has important physiological functions and has been implicated in several diseases. It is therefore of great physiological importance and clinical value to develop simple and efficient methods for measuring GSH levels. However, common used luminescent materials with short lifetimes inevitably generated background signal from the biological samples during light excitation. In this work, a GSH sensing method with ultralow background interference was developed using phosphorescent carbon dots (CDs). Introducing heteroatom and carbonyl group effectively activated the triplet state of phosphorescent CDs. Meanwhile, immobilization of CDs in SiO2 (CDs@SiO2) realized long-lived phosphorescent emission (lifetime of 1.54 s) in aqueous dispersion. Manganese dioxide (MnO2) quenched the phosphorescence of CDs@SiO2 via inner filter effect and triplet energy transfer, yet the phosphorescence was restored when GSH was present. The phosphorescence was recorded when the excitation source was turned off, which successfully eliminated the background fluorescence of biological samples. The work presented here will promote the fabrication of bioprobes with low background.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Phosphorescence
Carbon dots
Manganese dioxide
Glutathione
Background interference

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Phosphorescence detection of GSH based on CDs@SiO2/MnO2 complex
Design and synthesis of phosphorescent CDs
Optical properties of CDs and CDs@SiO2

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22106005), the Foundation of Education Department of Anhui Province (2022AH030047, 2022AH010023), and Innovative Training Program for College Students (202310360029).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic representation of the synthesis of CDs and CDs@SiO2; (b) TEM image of CDs; (c) XPS full survey spectrum of CDs; (d) FT-IR spectrum of CDs; (e) TEM image of CDs@SiO2; (f) XPS full survey spectrum of CDs@SiO2; (g) FT-IR spectrum of CDs@SiO2.

(a) 3D fluorescence spectra of CDs aqueous dispersion; (b) fluorescence emission spectra of CDs aqueous dispersion under different excitation wavelengths; (c) 3D phosphorescence spectra of CDs@SiO2 aqueous dispersion; (d) phosphorescence emission spectra of CDs@SiO2 aqueous dispersion under different excitation wavelengths.

(a) Photographs of CDs@SiO2 aqueous dispersion before and after turning off the 365 nm excitation light; (b) fluorescence spectra of CDs@SiO2 dispersion (0.15 mg in 1 mL water) before and after adding 50 μL of rabbit serum; (c) phosphorescence spectra of CDs@SiO2 dispersion (0.15 mg in 1 mL water) before and after adding 50 μL of rabbit serum.

(a) Schematic diagram of CDs@SiO2/MnO2 synthesis and detection of GSH; (b) and (c) TEM images of CDs@SiO2/MnO2 complex; (d) phosphorescence spectra of CDs@SiO2 dispersion (0.15 mg/mL) under 350 nm excitation with different amounts of MnO2; (e) phosphorescence intensity at 510 nm of CDs@SiO2 with different amounts of MnO2; (f) spectral overlap between UV–vis absorption of MnO2 and phosphorescence emission (under 350 nm excitation) of CDs@SiO2; (g) phosphorescence decay curves (emission at 510 nm with 350 nm excitation) of CDs@SiO2 (0.15 mg/mL) and CDs@SiO2/MnO2 aqueous dispersion (CDs@SiO2: 0.15 mg/mL, MnO2 0.3 mM).

(a) Phosphorescence recovery kinetics (intensity at 510 nm) of CDs@SiO2/MnO2 complex in the presence of 200 μM GSH; (b) UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs@SiO2/MnO2 complex in the presence of 200 μM of GSH for different incubation time; (c) phosphorescence decay curves of CDs@SiO2/MnO2 complex with addition of 50 and 300 μM of GSH; (d) phosphorescence emission spectra of CDs@SiO2/MnO2 complexes with the addition of different concentrations of GSH; (e) the relationship between phosphorescence recovery of CDs@SiO2/MnO2 and the concentration of GSH (I0: phosphorescence intensity of CDs@SiO2/MnO2 without GSH, I: phosphorescence intensity of CDs@SiO2/MnO2 in the presence of GSH); (f) phosphorescence response of CDs@SiO2/MnO2 to GSH and interferents (200 μM for interferent concentration and GSH concentration, except 10 μM for Vc and 50 μM for Cys).
Results for the GSH assay in blood samples.

head.ce:title: Employing phosphorescent carbon dots@silica for glutathione sensing with low background interference

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Anhui University of Technology, Maanshan, Anhui 243032, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Anhui University of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Anhui University of Technology, Maanshan, Anhui 243032, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Glutathione (GSH) has important physiological functions and has been implicated in several diseases. It is therefore of great physiological importance and clinical value to develop simple and efficient methods for measuring GSH levels. However, common used luminescent materials with short lifetimes inevitably generated background signal from the biological samples during light excitation. In this work, a GSH sensing method with ultralow background interference was developed using phosphorescent carbon dots (CDs). Introducing heteroatom and carbonyl group effectively activated the triplet state of phosphorescent CDs. Meanwhile, immobilization of CDs in SiO2 (CDs@SiO2) realized long-lived phosphorescent emission (lifetime of 1.54 s) in aqueous dispersion. Manganese dioxide (MnO2) quenched the phosphorescence of CDs@SiO2 via inner filter effect and triplet energy transfer, yet the phosphorescence was restored when GSH was present. The phosphorescence was recorded when the excitation source was turned off, which successfully eliminated the background fluorescence of biological samples. The work presented here will promote the fabrication of bioprobes with low background.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Glutathione (GSH), a typical reducing non-protein thiol in organisms, serves various important physiological functions [1–3]. GSH regulates the intracellular redox balance and protects the cells from free radicals induced DNA or RNA damage [4,5]. Notably, GSH level in human body change associated with many diseases, such as Alzheimer’s disease, liver injury, Parkinson’s disease, human immunodeficiency virus (HIV) infection, and even cancer [6–8]. Therefore, the development of simple and efficient method to detect GSH levels has important physiological significance and clinical value.

Optical methods are commonly used for detection of GSH. Among the established techniques, luminescent nanoprobes with high sensitivity and good stability have been used for sensing of GSH [9–12]. Since GSH itself has no fluorescent signal, the current luminescent probes for GSH utilize the strong reduction of GSH. Typically, Liu’s group using energy transfer quenching between luminescent upconversion nanoparticles (UCNPs) and manganese dioxide (MnO2) constructed a rapid and selective sensing platform for GSH [13]. Employing the reducing reaction between GSH and MnO2 to break the quenching of UCNPs, the GSH was detected rapidly and selectively in aqueous solutions and living cells. Based on this principle, many nanostructured luminescent materials have been developed with MnO2 to build GSH sensors, including fluorescent carbon dots [14], fluorescent conjugated polymer nanoparticles [15], boron nitride quantum dots [16], etc. However, these materials exhibit very short luminescent lifetimes (in the nanosecond range). Inevitably, the background fluorescence of biological sample coexist during the light excitation.

Phosphorescent materials generate long lifetime emissions which originate from radiation transition of excited triplet state to ground singlet state [17]. Significantly, room temperature phosphorescent carbon dots (CDs) have emerged in recent years with the remarkable property that they can continue to emit light for a period of time after the excitation light source is turned off [18,19]. However, the phosphorescence from triplet excited state is more easily quenched by external environment, such as collisions with solvent or dissolved oxygen [20,21]. Recent studies have shown that immobilization of room temperature phosphorescent CDs in silica [22], inorganic salts [23], and other matrices can effectively protect the triplet states of CDs, thus realizing phosphorescent emissions of CDs in aqueous dispersion. It is worth mentioning that the long lifetime of CDs can effectively overcome the interference of background fluorescence by recording the phosphorescence after turning off the excitation light source. Therefore, the room temperature phosphorescent CDs are an ideal luminescent material for bioanalysis [24–27].

Herein, we developed a fast and effective method to synthesis phosphorescent CDs using 2-imidazolidinone and phosphorus pentoxide as raw materials. By embedding the CDs in a silica matrix, bright green phosphorescence that remains visible to the naked eye for about 10 s was achieved in aqueous media. The formation of GSH-responsive MnO2 nanosheets on the surface of CDs@SiO2 was achieved in situ by reducing KMnO4 under weak acidic conditions. Due to the long lifetime of phosphorescence emission of CDs@SiO2, the fluorescence background from coexisting species in biological fluids was successfully eliminated. Therefore, a sensitive and selective nanoprobe for GSH was established. The nanoprobe was used to examine blood samples, and it produced accurate results for the GSH assay.

Tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS), 2-imidazolidinone, ammonia, serine, tryptophan (Trp), NaCl, KCl, CaCl2, and MgCl2 were purchased from Shanghai Titan Scientific Co., Ltd. Ethanol, isopropanol, KMnO4, MnCl2, Na2SO4, glutamic acid (Glu), alanine (Ala), histidine (His), proline (Pro), glycine (Gly), cysteine (Cys), and glucose were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Phosphorus pentoxide (P2O5), vitamin C (Vc), sodium tetraborate decahydrate (Na2B4O7∙10 H2O), 2-morpholinoethanesulfonic acid (MES), and N-(2-hydroxyethyl)piperazine-N’-(2-ethanesulfonic acid) (HEPES) were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. Na2CO3, rabbit serum were obtained from Beijing InnoChem Science & Technology Co., Ltd. Glutathione (GSH) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Life Science & Technology Co., Ltd. All reagents and materials were used directly without further purification.

Firstly, 8 mmol of 2-imidazolidinone was mixed with 8 mmol of P2O5 in a 50 mL beaker, and then 2 mL of ultrapure water was added. The above mixture was heated with an electric furnace until the color of mixture changed to golden yellow, which took about 6 min. After cooling down naturally, 10 mL of ultrapure water was added and the obtained dispersion was neutralized with Na2CO3. The resulting solution was centrifuged at 7000 rpm for 10 min, and the supernatant was filtered through a syringe-driven filter (0.22 μm) to remove large carbon particles. Finally, the dispersion was purified by dialysis and phosphorescent CDs were obtained by freeze-drying.

In brief, 200 μL of the CDs dispersion (14 mg/mL) was mixed with 20 mL of ultrapure water, the pH of the dispersion was adjusted to 9–10 with ammonia. Then 3.2 mL of TEOS was added and the mixture was stirred vigorously at 80 °C for 24 h. After cooling down naturally, the solution was filtered through a syringe-driven filter (0.22 μm) and the CDs@SiO2 was obtained by precipitation with isopropanol. The product was washed several times with isopropanol and ethanol for further use.

Typically, 2 mL of CDs@SiO2 aqueous dispersion (1.5 mg/mL) was mixed with 18 mL of MES buffer (0.1 M, pH=6), and then different amounts of KMnO4 (100 mM) were added. The above mixture was sonicated for 30 min to obtain a brown dispersion. During which time the KMnO4 was reduced into MnO2 nanosheets and deposited on the surface of CDs@SiO2. The CDs@SiO2/MnO2 complex was collected by centrifugation, washed several times with ultrapure water, and the final products were re-dispersed in 20 mL of HEPES buffer (10 mM, pH=7.2).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Phosphorescence detection of GSH based on CDs@SiO2/MnO2 complex
ce:section.ce:para: Typically, 500 μL of CDs@SiO2/MnO2 (0.15 mg/mL) and different amounts of GSH were mixed with HEPES buffer (10 mM, pH=7.2) to a final volume of 1 mL. The mixture was incubated at 37 °C for 10 min. After that, the phosphorescence spectra were recorded with a spectrometer under the excitation of 350 nm.

Whole blood sample from volunteer was collected in heparin anticoagulation tubes, and 20 μL of serine-borate complex (100 mM) was added to 1 mL of whole blood to inhibit γ-glutamyl transpeptidase (GGT) activity [28,29]. The samples were then centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 15 min at room temperature to remove blood cells. The resulting supernatant was diluted 5-fold with HEPES buffer (10 mM, pH=7.2), and 250 μL of diluted plasma was added to the sensing system (500 μL of CDs@SiO2/MnO2 and 250 μL of HEPES buffer). After incubation at 37 °C for 10 min, the phosphorescence spectra were recorded under the excitation of 350 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design and synthesis of phosphorescent CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The phosphorescent CDs were prepared using 2-imidazolidinone and phosphorus pentoxide as raw materials by heating-carbonization in an electric furnace ( Fig. 1a). 2-imidazolidinone was chosen as the carbon source because of the existence the nitrogen element and carbonyl group (CO), which can promote the intersystem crossing (ISC) process and enable CDs to produce effective phosphorescence emission. Phosphorus pentoxide has strong dehydrating property, which contributes to the formation of CDs when co-heated with 2-imidazolidinone. Meanwhile, the introduction of phosphorus into CDs can further promote the ISC process and improve the phosphorescence emission of CDs [30]. To prove our conception, 8 mmol of 2-imidazolidinone and 8 mmol of phosphorus pentoxide were mixed with 2 mL of water, and then the mixture was heated and boiled. When heated for 3 min, the mixture formed a clear and transparent solution. Organic small molecules undergo cross-linking and condensation reactions to produce polymer-like intermediate. When heated for 6 min, the mixture boiled vigorously and formed a golden-yellow solution. During this process, the intermediate gradually generate sp2/sp3 hybrid carbon core and polymer chains shell. After 15 min of heating, the mixture was completely black and a large amount of black solid was produced (Fig. S1). The results confirmed that the designed method can be used for rapid preparation of CDs.

Previous studies have shown that excessive carbonization is not conducive for long-lived phosphorescence in CDs [21,31]. Here, we selected the samples from heating of 6 min for detailed investigation. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image (Fig. 1b) shows that the synthesized CDs are well dispersed and have a near-spherical morphology with an average particle size of 1.81 ± 0.37 nm (Fig. S2). HRTEM image (Fig. S3) shows that the CDs are semi-crystalline with a crystal plane spacing of 0.22 nm, which corresponds to the (100) plane of graphene. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) full survey spectrum (Fig. 1c) indicates that four elements, C, O, N, and P, are mainly present in the CDs, with atomic contents of 46.06%, 33.65%, 11.75%, and 8.54%, (Table S1) respectively. The high-resolution XPS spectra of C 1 s show that the CDs contain C-C (284.5 eV), C-P (285.2 eV), C-N (286.2 eV) and CO (287.8 eV) bonds (Fig. S4a) [32]. The O 1 s spectra suggest the presence of PO (530.6 eV), C-OH/C-O-C (531.7 eV), and P-O (532.6 eV) bonds (Fig. S4b). The N 1 s can be attributed to the C-NC (399.3 eV), N-(C)3 (400.4 eV), and N-H (401.2 eV) bonds (Fig. S4c). The P 2p spectra (Fig. S4d) have two peaks at 133.1 eV and 134.0 eV, which can be attributed to P-O and P-N/P-C bonds, respectively [30]. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum (Fig. 1d) confirms that the surface of CDs is enriched with a large number of functional groups. Among them, the broad absorption peaks at 3600–3100 cm−1 can be attributed to the O-H/N-H stretching vibration. The peak at 1638 cm−1 corresponds to the CO/CN stretching vibration, and the peak of the N-H bending vibration is located at 1542 cm−1. Peaks at 1455 and 1235 cm−1 are attributed to the C-H and C-N vibration, respectively. The peaks at 1105 and 884 cm−1 could be associated with the P-O stretching vibration [32,33].

To realize the phosphorescence emission in aqueous dispersion, the CDs were immobilized in SiO2 (Fig. 1a). TEM image in Fig. 1e indicates that the CDs@SiO2 is spherical in shape with an average particle size of 13.98±1.88 nm (Fig. S5). XPS full survey spectrum (Fig. 1f, Table S2) and FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 1g) show that the surface of CDs is dominated by Si-O groups, demonstrating that the CDs were successfully capped by SiO2 [34].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of CDs and CDs@SiO2
ce:section.ce:para: The CDs and CDs@SiO2 showed remarkable optical properties in the aqueous dispersion. The UV-Vis absorption spectra were tested firstly (Fig. S6). The absorption of CDs was mainly concentrated in the UV region, showing two obvious absorption peaks at 286 nm and 343 nm. When CDs were immobilized in SiO2, the absorption peaks were broadened and there were no obvious absorption peaks, which was attributed to the low content of CDs in SiO2. CDs display obvious fluorescence emission, which can be seen from the three-dimensional (3D) fluorescence spectra ( Fig. 2a). CDs have two excitation bands, with the maximum excitation wavelengths of 280 and 350 nm, which are consistent with the UV absorption spectrum. When the excitation wavelength was changed, the fluorescence emission peak of CDs remained essentially unchanged, while the fluorescence emission peak was located at 405 nm under the optimal excitation of 350 nm (Fig. 2b). However, no phosphorescence emission could be detected from the CDs themselves in aqueous dispersion (Fig. S7), which was mainly due to the phosphorescence quenching caused by collision effect of solvent and dissolved oxygen [20,21]. Immobilization of CDs in SiO2 essentially did not change the fluorescent properties of CDs, but resulted in significant phosphorescence emission from CDs. As shown in Fig. 2c, CDs@SiO2 exhibits a strong excitation independent phosphorescence emission peak under the excitation range of 280–300 nm. With the optimal excitation at 350 nm, the phosphorescence peak of CDs@SiO2 is located at 510 nm (Fig. 2d).

The synthesized CDs@SiO2 not only possesses strong room temperature phosphorescence emission, but also has a long phosphorescence lifetime. As shown in Fig. 3a, the aqueous dispersion of CDs@SiO2 exhibits blue fluorescence under 365 nm UV irradiation. After turning off the UV lamp, the sample emits bright green phosphorescence that can last for about 10 s to naked eyes. Phosphorescence decay curve (Fig. S8) and fitting (Table S3) reveal that the lifetime at 510 nm of CDs@SiO2 is about 1.54 s. The high efficiency of the phosphorescence emission can be attributed to the following reasons: (i) the selected raw materials contain carbonyl, heteroatom N and P, all of which can effectively enhance the ISC process to activate the triplet state of CDs; (ii) the CDs embedded in silica hinders the collision of solvent molecules and dissolved oxygen, which protects the triple excited state of CDs and allows the radiative transitions of triple excited state to produce phosphorescence. This long-lived emission gained an advantage that the phosphorescence intensity could be recorded with the excitation source turned off for a certain period of time (at which time the short-lived fluorescence emission has completely decayed). Thus, the organism’s background fluorescence interference is successfully eliminated. To validate this approach, we tested the fluorescence spectra of CDs@SiO2 followed by the addition of rabbit serum samples, which showed a significant enhancement of the collected fluorescence signal (Fig. 3b). This is due to the fact that the serum contains proteins, porphyrines, etc., which have some fluorescence interference (Fig. S9) [35,36]. In contrast, in the phosphorescence mode, the introduction of rabbit serum had almost no effect on the phosphorescence signal of CDs@SiO2, confirming that the short-lived background fluorescence interference had been eliminated (Fig. 3c).

The phosphorescent CDs@SiO2 can effectively overcome the background fluorescence, however the phosphorescence signal of CDs@SiO2 needs “on-off” response to the GSH. To this end, the CDs@SiO2/MnO2 was further synthesized to quench the phosphorescence of CDs@SiO2. The in situ growth of MnO2 nanosheets on the surface of CDs@SiO2 was achieved using the reduction of KMnO4 under weak acidic conditions ( Fig. 4a). The color of the CDs@SiO2 dispersion changed from colorless to brownish-yellow after coating by MnO2 (Fig. S10). As presented in the TEM image (Fig. 4b), the MnO2-coated CDs@SiO2 undergo aggregation and are no longer in a monodisperse state. From the high magnification image (Fig. 4c), there is an obvious wrinkled structure, which proves that the MnO2 is an ultrathin nanosheet. Furthermore, it was confirmed that MnO2 was uniformly composited with CDs@SiO2 using energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS) mapping (Fig. S11). The coating of MnO2 could significantly quench the phosphorescence of CDs@SiO2, and the emission intensity of CDs@SiO2 was gradually decreased with the increase of the amount of MnO2 (Fig. 4d and e). When the concentration of MnO2 was 0.3 mM, its quenching efficiency reached as high as 94%.

In order to investigate the phosphorescence quenching mechanism of MnO2 on CDs@SiO2, the absorption spectrum of MnO2 was tested (Fig. 4f). MnO2 has a strong absorption peak at 350 nm, which overlaps with the excitation of CDs@SiO2. Since MnO2 uniformly covers the surface of CDs@SiO2, this inevitably leads to a part of the excitation light being absorbed by MnO2, resulting in phosphorescence quenching by the inner filter effect [37]. On the other hand, there is a spectral overlap between the absorption of MnO2 and the emission of CDs@SiO2, which has the possibility to lead to the excited state energy transfer [25,38]. Comparison of the phosphorescent emission lifetimes of CDs@SiO2 and CDs@SiO2/MnO2 (Fig. 4g and Table S3), MnO2 significantly shortens the lifetime of CDs@SiO2 (from 1542.88 ms to 860.25 ms when 0.3 mM of MnO2 was added). Therefore, MnO2 serves as an energy acceptor for the excited triplet state energy of CDs@SiO2. A combination of inner filter effect and energy transfer leads to the effective phosphorescence quenching of CDs@SiO2.

GSH, as a powerful reductant, is capable of reducing MnO2 to form manganese ions (Mn2+), which in turn dismantles the MnO2 nanosheets on the surface of CDs@SiO2. Due to the addition of GSH, the phosphorescence emissions of CDs@SiO2 are restored (Fig. 4a). To the HEPES buffer solution (10 mM pH=7.2) containing CDs@SiO2/MnO2 (0.15 mg/mL) and 200 μM of GSH, the mixture was incubated at 37 °C and the phosphorescence spectra were measured at different time. The phosphorescence intensity of CDs@SiO2 was gradually restored with the increase of the incubation time and maintained after 10 min ( Fig. 5a). The UV–vis absorption spectra (Fig. 5b) also indicated that the absorbance of MnO2 was gradually decreased with the increase of incubation time, confirming the disruption of the MnO2. Meanwhile, the addition of GSH restored the lifetime of the CDs@SiO2 (Fig. 5c and Table S3). Subsequently, under the condition of optimal incubation time (10 min), we introduced different concentrations of GSH into the sensing system. The phosphorescence intensity of the probe was gradually increased with the raising of the concentration of GSH. In the range of 3–200 μM, the phosphorescence recovery showed a good linear relationship with GSH concentration, and the detection limit was estimated to be 1.7 μM (Fig. 5d and e). Compared to the reported methods, including chromatography, electrophoresis, electrochemical sensor, and Raman spectroscopy, the designed CDs@SiO2/MnO2 system was able to achieve the quantitative detection of GSH in an effective manner (Table S4). Moreover, phosphorescent probe has lower detection limit than traditional fluorescent materials (Table S5).

To verify the selectivity of the proposed method, we tested the phosphorescence response of common biomolecules and inorganic ions on the CDs@SiO2/MnO2 probe. As shown in Fig. 5f, reducing molecules, such as glucose, cysteine, Vc, etc., had negligible effect on the phosphorescent probe. In addition, common inorganic ions in biological samples exhibited no effects on the phosphorescence of CDs@SiO2. The results confirmed the specificity of the reaction between MnO2 and GSH.

Finally, human blood was analysed for GSH content using the developed CDs@SiO2/MnO2 sensing system. Whole blood samples collected from healthy volunteer was treated with serine borate complex to inhibit the γ-glutamyl transpeptidase (GGT) activity. After centrifugation to remove the blood cells, the GSH-containing plasma samples were obtained [28,29]. Similarly, there was a strong background fluorescence in the diluted plasma samples, but this could be eliminated in the phosphorescence assay mode (Fig. S12). The addition of the plasma samples to the CDs@SiO2/MnO2 system induced a significant recovery of phosphorescence. As shown in Table 1, by adding 10, 50, 80, and 100 μM of GSH standard solutions, the obtained recoveries ranged from 91.72% to 108.2%, indicating that the proposed method has good accuracy. Therefore, CDs@SiO2 with excellent phosphorescent properties is a promising nanomaterial that can be used to prepare luminescent probes for low background interference analysis of complex components.

In conclusion, we have constructed a CDs@SiO2/MnO2 nanoprobe for the sensitive and selective detection of GSH. In the designed probe, the phosphorescent CDs@SiO2 exhibited ultralong lifetime which can overcome the background interference. MnO2 nanosheets on the surface of CDs@SiO2 effectively quenched the long-lived phosphorescence through inner filter effect and triplet energy transfer. Moreover, the GSH can be recognized through the redox reaction between MnO2 and GSH. The destruction of MnO2 nanosheets caused phosphorescence recovery of CDs@SiO2. With such low background sensing platform, GSH in blood samples was successfully analysed. Using phosphorescent CDs to develop nanoprobes will generate tremendous opportunities for biosensors.

Xinyi Li: Writing - original draft, Investigation. Wanqing Liang: Investigation，Formal analysis. Yawen Gao: Methodology，Conceptualization. Yongqin Shi: Validation. Cheng Zhang: Writing - original draft, Supervision，Project administration. Kui Zhang: Writing - original draft，Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22106005), the Foundation of Education Department of Anhui Province (2022AH030047, 2022AH010023), and Innovative Training Program for College Students (202310360029).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2024.135680.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Rational design of reversible fluorescent probes for live-cell imaging and quantification of fast glutathione dynamics
Nat. Chem.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A rapid fluorescence “switch-on” assay for glutathione detection by using carbon dots-MnO2 nanocomposites
Biosens. Bioelectron.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Q.Y. Cai, J. Li, J. Ge, L. Zhang, Y.L. Hu, Z.H. Li, L.B. Qu, A rapid fluorescence “switch-on” assay for glutathione detection by using carbon dots-MnO2 nanocomposites, Biosens. Bioelectron. 72 (2015) 31-36.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Quantum dot-based off/on probe for detection of glutathione
J. Phys. Chem. C

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Banerjee, S. Kar, J.M. Perez, S. Santra, Quantum dot-based off/on probe for detection of glutathione, J. Phys. Chem. C 113 (2009) 9659-9663.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Selective getection of cysteine and glutathione using gold nanorods
J. Am. Chem. Soc.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Selective detection of the reduced form of glutathione (GSH) over the oxidized (GSSG) form using a combination of glutathione reductase and a Tb(III)-Cyclen maleimide based lanthanide luminescent ‘switch on’ assay
J. Am. Chem. Soc.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B.K. McMahon, T. Gunnlaugsson, Selective detection of the reduced form of glutathione (GSH) over the oxidized (GSSG) form using a combination of glutathione reductase and a Tb(III)-Cyclen maleimide based lanthanide luminescent ‘switch on’ assay, J. Am. Chem. Soc. 134 (2012) 10725-10728.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Glutathione detection based on peroxidase-like activity of Co3O4-montmorillonite nanocomposites

Nanoscale

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Xinyi Li is currently pursuing his M.S. degree at Anhui University of Technology. His current field of research interest is luminescent sensor.
Wanqing Liang is currently pursuing his M.S. degree at Anhui University of Technology. His current field of research interest is carbon dots.
Yawen Gao is currently pursuing her M.S. degree at Anhui University of Technology. Her current field of research interest is luminescent sensor.
Yongqin Shi is an undergraduate student at Anhui University of Technology. Her current field of research interest is luminescent sensor.

Cheng Zhang is associate professor at Anhui University of Technology. He got a PhD from University of Science and Technology of China in 2017. His current research interests are focused on the design and synthesis of luminescent materials for environmental and sensing applications.

Kui Zhang is professor at Anhui University of Technology in China. He got a PhD from University of Science and Technology of China in 2011 and worked as a research assistant at the Institute of Intelligent Machines, Chinese Academy of Sciences (2011–2015), and as a research fellow at the Center of Super-Diamond and Advanced Films (COSDAF) in the City University of Hong Kong (2015–2017). His research interests are in analytical chemistry, nanotechnology, and the development of functional nanomaterials for chemo/bio sensing, environmental and energy application.
